fingerprint: Coupling constant κ = 10.9/6.5 depends entirely on globe-predicted eclipse circumstances — dome has no independent eclipse path prediction
globe_value: Eclipse timing, path, and coverage fraction computed from Besselian elements (spherical astronomy + Keplerian orbits). Mohe totality 1997-03-09 and BOU 93.5% coverage 2017-08-21 both require globe ephemeris.
dome_value: κ = 1.67 nT/μGal treated as fundamental coupling constant, but both numerator (10.9 nT at BOU) and denominator (6.5 μGal at Mohe) were measured during eclipses located in time and space by globe astronomy. Station coordinates are WGS84.
match_precision: N/A — infrastructure dependence, not mathematical constant match
why_it_matters: The dome model (local sun at 5,733 km, local moon at 2,534 km) cannot independently predict when or where eclipses occur — eclipse shadow paths depend on the Sun-Moon-Earth geometry that only spherical astronomy computes. Without globe ephemeris, the dome cannot identify which data points to use for κ. The E-PRED coverage fraction is itself a spherical-geometry quantity (angular overlap of disc luminaries as seen from a point on a sphere). On a flat disc with luminaries at fixed altitudes, eclipse shadow geometry would be fundamentally different — the shadow would not trace the narrow band predicted by spherical models. Additionally, κ is not derived from any dome parameter (disc_radius, H(r), n(r)) — it is purely empirical, making it doubly parasitic on globe infrastructure.
suggested_text: The coupling constant κ = 1.67 nT/μGal is not derived from any dome parameter — it is the ratio of two measurements taken during eclipses whose timing, paths, and coverage fractions were computed by standard spherical astronomy. The dome model has no independent mechanism to predict eclipse paths; with a local sun at 5,733 km and a local moon at 2,534 km, the shadow geometry would differ radically from what standard ephemeris computes. Without the globe's Besselian elements, the dome cannot even identify which observations contribute to κ.
notes: Lower confidence than mathematical-constant fingerprints (WIN-001 π/2, WIN-003 a=πR) because this is an infrastructure dependence argument rather than a precise numerical match. However, the dependence is fundamental: the phenomenon being studied (eclipse effects) can only be located in time and space using globe astronomy. Already partially covered in our E-PRED analysis but not explicitly connected to WIN-011's coupling constant provenance.
